pages.full_text:
at Ulm. Jewish window, Goose poking gargoyle, choir carvings, central altar. American Meier designed modern town hall. Difficult lunch - making decisions in a foreign language.
	We drove on through lovely grassy fields of Bavaria to __Ellessee__ an old campsite on a lake.
	Aitrang / Allgau
	It's well laid out with good clean showers. You have to pay to swim in the lake. Art classes - arty things round campsite, Metal work, glass, poems! Lights in showers painted. Clean air. Enormous stones marking levels of pitches. What was there originally? Fresh bread at breakfast. It rained in the evening.
	~~Friday~~ Thursday -
	We pass dinky red trains. Bavaria is very beautiful. Hot terrible traffic jams caused by trivial roadworks. German motorway, too dirty. Late morning we pass thro Austria. Past Innsbruck. on to Bolsano. A long day's driving. We keep pink Dolomite Mountains on our left. Fruit trees. V. fertile valley. Castle.
	Lake Garda comes in sight & we join the
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence (continuation from previous spread). 'Friday' is crossed out and 'Thursday' written above — Thursday is the intended reading. 'Ellessee' is underlined and is a lake/campsite in Bavaria — could read 'Elbsee' or 'Ellessee'; the Allgäu reference (Aitrang / Allgau) is consistent with Elbsee near Rettenberg in the Allgäu region. 'Allgau' may be a shorthand for 'Allgäu'. 'Bolsano' is the diary's spelling of Bolzano (Italian city, South Tyrol). 'Innsbruck' and Dolomites confirm the route south through Austria into northern Italy. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume covers 2003–2019. A small decorative mark (asterisks or star) appears after 'lovely' before the next line. Text ends mid-sentence at page bottom, continuing on right page.

pages.full_text:
queue of traffic down the hill. We try 4 different campsites, all full. In the end we go to [?Bolsari] Transit which we think will be just a lay by but instead it turns out to be a Check-in/post site just for camping vans. Amazing. No lights at night. Only the van width next to us for table & chairs. Sat nice people. The van opposite have a kid russel + border terrier. They give us their fresh left-over bread for our supper. The woman next to us has a 10 year old son, Felix. I've never seen a single parent woman camping before, on her own. She's from Dresden. She teaches circus, she [illegible] in Sheffield + London.
	She + son with [illegible]
	[?Gloucester]
	[?Winchsea]
	[illegible]
	[illegible]
	[sticker: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore]
	Friday we [?sat] after breakfast. V. beautiful.
photo_captions: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore
pages.notes: Handwritten page number '17' visible in top-right corner. Text begins mid-sentence, continuing from left page. '[?Bolsari] Transit' is the campsite/transit area name — reading of 'Bolsari' is uncertain; could be 'Bardolino', 'Boscari', or another Lake Garda locality. The large oval sticker (tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it) covers a significant portion of the lower right half of the page, obscuring text beneath and to the left. The website areatransit.it confirms the Transit site is an Italian campervan transit area. Several lines of text to the left of and below the sticker are partially or fully obscured; '[?Gloucester]' and '[?Winchsea]' appear to be place names in England mentioned by the woman from Dresden — she may have said she performs/lives in those towns. The sticker photo appears to show a large lake-shore parking/camping area consistent with Lake Garda. 'Felix' is the name of the neighbouring woman's son, not a place. A word after 'we' before 'after breakfast' is not fully legible — possibly 'sat', 'set off', or 'swam'. The next spread (B_0194) picks up 'off in the van left at Garda' confirming this is Lake Garda area.
